item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I initially didn't like this SCP - because it conflicts with my headcanon that all the anomalous stuff of the Foundation-verse is natural to the multiverse.

Like, every universe has something a little weird about it - the Foundation-verse is just a place where the barriers between realities have frayed, so there's been a bit of cross-pollination between realities and that's why the Foundation-verse has LOTS of different kinds of weird shit.

But… how can they be sure there are no anomalies and no Foundation equivalent?

The US government-B says they know nothing about anomalies or a Foundation equivalent. However… if Foundation-B was doing there jobs properly, the politicians wouldn't know, would they?

I love the idea that there are anomalies and a Foundation - it's just their Foundation is much more effective than ours. Every potentially dangerous SCP (including ones that are barely contained in baseline) was either neutralised or permanently contained, and the few anomalies that are still uncontained are pretty much harmless/unnoticeable.

Because they're more effective, they don't need to reach out for government co-operation and, aside from a few paranoid types in secret service agencies, nobody knows they even exist, let alone what their purpose is.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like the way you think! Now how am I gonna create an Interdimensional portal…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: wait is it our world.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Downvoted. I don't like how it seems to imply the Foundation is not in our real world. It's like when Marvel invented its comics took place on Earth-616 and not our own. A parallel universe where the anomalous doesn't exist shoud be eerily different, not-right in an uncanny valley way.

Also, didn't care much for the liason being made with the USA. Shouldn't it be the UN? Or did the portal just happen to take you to the US, instead of (say) Mozambique? What about the Unusual Incidents Unit, it doesn't exist there?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Maybe there should be an incident where an instance of SCP-939 was found to give some motivation to 1485-A.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The point is lost on me. The article operates on the assumption that being anomalous is inherently bad, and in this sense, it relies on the reader's own experiences with the predominantly unsettling nature of mainlist SCPs. However, this isn't effectively demonstrated in the article. In fact, it's basically stated that, at the macro-scale, anomalies don't make much of a difference. If all the anomalies of the Foundationverse, including all the GoIs and PoIs and everything, were to vanish in an instant, nothing much would change. This is antithetical to the conceit of the article and it makes the actions of the deserting employees seem a bit much. They're willing to abandon their old lives, including their families, because there's a place where anomalies don't exist? If there was some substantial attempt to show how anomalies make the world a bad place, I could maybe sympathise more. But right know, it comes across as strangely overreactive on the part of the Foundation staff.

The interview doesn't help much in this regard, specifically the final few lines. It's pretty laughable that the agent ostensibly associates everything they can't understand with the anomalous (in the sense of the Foundationverse). The agent seems incredibly naive for it - has she never lived in the real world? There are many things that don't make sense, but that doesn't automatically mean that the Foundation contains them all.

It's an interesting premise, but you've barely explored the implications. And the set-up is just too contrived (how can the Foundation know that the entire dimension is devoid of anomalies? That's like going to a dimension where Earth is devoid of life and saying "oh, sterile universe, I guess"). You've also not really addressed what an 'anomaly' actually entails, and how frequently anomalies actually occur in the Foundationverse. These are questions that I feel need to be addressed/cleared up, considering the premise.

All in all, it's just too half-baked for me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Looking at this again, I was thinking the interview log could use some minor clean-up. I like to think I've gotten better at writing dialogue since this first went up.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Well, if we consider the poster thinking of a truly infinite universe…

… the answer is going to be "the chances of Milo writing a SCP into real life are infinity to one". Or we shouldn't even bet on it.

Detailed explanation
Gimme all the Tylenol in the pharmacy for the headache I just got!

To start, we first assume that there are an infinite amount of universes, with infinite variations of how they came to be, physical properties, etc. Then, we start narrowing it down to just universes that, let's say, formed life. That cuts the universes we need to consider in half. However, that's still infinity, it's just one half as large as all the infinite universes. Then we narrow it down to ones where humans exist, ones where Earth exists and so on until we nestle into the much-smaller-yet-still-infinite group of universes where both we and the SCP verse resides in. While all this doesn't have any direct bearing on the question, we need it so we can keep in mind that there are actually larger and smaller infinities.

The question if Milo does write a SCP here, we have the following possibilites: Milo successfully writes in SCPS to our universe or Milo doesn't write them in.

What's that? Only 50-50 you say? No, actually, it's not.

While there's an infinite amount of ways where Milo can write a SCP into Real Life, it's a much smaller infinity where they don't. Because under the codifer "Milo doesn't write in SCPs," there are the infinities in categories of everything from "Bob down the street from Milo is the only one who will do it" to "This universe will never have a SCP in it" to "SCPs will never reach Earth" to "Milo never correctly writes in the one that would cross over", as well as all the various other infinities included in that prevent Milo from successfully writing in a SCP.

Yes, they all count because we're comparing them against one codifier- where Milo successfully writes a SCP into real-life. For Milo to succeed, this universe MUST be connected to the SCP verse, AND it must be possible to write a SCP into the real life, AND Milo is the one to do it. So long as one of those three is not true, it won't happen. And considering all the universes that are not connected to the SCP verse (like just about all mass media, to start with), all the universes where a SCP never interacts with Earth, all the universes where humans never meet a SCP, all the universes where it is not possible to write a SCP into real life, all the ones where Milo is not the one to do it, et cetera, that's a much larger infinity than the specific scenario that we defined.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think they are saying that if an infinite number of possible universes exist, this includes all imaginary universes, including imaginary universes that are in contact with all other universes including ours. Since this isn't observed, they are postulating that there is some force that makes it impossible for those universes to communicate.

However this is flawed, because an infinite number of universes does not actually mean that all imaginary universes exist, rather that an infinite number of universes with normal distribution of probabilities across only possible events exists.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You can have a infinite number of apples and zero oranges. The many worlds hypothesis only requires that quantum-scale events with nonzero probabilities occur in divergent timelines, not that impossible events ever occur, or even that macro-scale events cause divergence.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Now that I think about it, maybe there's some kind of supernatural force preventing anything, including the portal to my story universe, from penetrating our "normal" universe

SCPs aren't real, though. I'm not sure how "supernatural" the force of "the articles only exist because non-anomalous, non-magical people wrote them" is, since it's effectively common sense?

That said, I had to read the above post several times to piece together what was being said, so it's entirely possible that everything about it was just several layers too meta for me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I wish that that was there so that WE COULD HAVE SCIPS!!!!!!1 But seriously, if the Multiple Worlds Theory is correct, then writing that would let us have scips!!! Hell, one time, I started writing a makeshift story on paper, kind of like what they write for testing with SCP-826 (The Sword That Shoots Laser Beams When You Swing It) that would describe a dimension that only consists of one room with an object in it that allows the holder to visit fictional universes, such as, but not limited to, ones that are made up on the spot like this one in order to get cool supernatural stuff, but I never finished the story. Now that I think about it, maybe there's some kind of supernatural force preventing anything, including the portal to my story universe, from penetrating our "normal" universe, which is preventing my portal from appearing by making me procrastinate finishing the story!!! O.O (Yeah, I need to get out more XD).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Sorry, but that ending is weak as fuck and so obvious there was hardly any point in actually writing it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Awesome idea, Fair delivery. +1
Squiggit touched on a lot of my thoughts, but basically, it could have been so much more with one or more tweaks.
First, the Foundation would be less confident that the other world was what it seemed. The uniformity of defection should make them suspect something memetic (even if it isn't there). The lack of observed anomalies could also mean that they are present, but more "black swan events" than "the natural order," that the Foundation-equivalent does a great job, that the GOC-equivalent already did a superb job of destroying the anomalous, or that Something wants that world to look Very Tempting. The Foundation would leave room for paranoid caution in the report, and as an IRL work of fiction, that ambiguity would make it more exciting.
Also, and a few people touched on this already, Earth-Prime isn't anywhere close to anomaly free now, and a lot of it is the Foundation's fault! It's not just SCP-1485. The Foundationverse is flooded with different odd energies and anomalous broadcasts, which would trickle through the portal, but those aren't the Foundation's fault. Dozens, maybe even hundreds of SCPs on the site refer to or imply ubiquitous anomalies affecting everybody in-setting, or at least all Foundation personnel. Even the non-creepy stuff, like previous amnestic treatments or exposure to any of the infohazard/secrecy containment memes used by the Foundation. Just standard antimemetics would infect the other world like bacteria taken to a sterile, global petri dish. Those memes will eventually mutate in a new environment, and they are so vastly more robust and infectious than mundane memes! And they're being spread by every defector that goes to ground. 20+ years is long enough for government analysts on Earth-Prime to realize this, and the icy reception is because anything the Foundation does will only accelerate the problem.
And at the highest levels, the Foundation probably knows it. In time, Earth-Prime will have problems that they NEED the Foundation to help with, and will invite the Foundation in. When that happens, the Foundation will be much more powerful (existing in a second, mirror world with no GOIs), and will have an Earth-sized and -shaped life-raft (or prison colony, or dump site), should it ever be needed. I could even imagine a cynical version of the O5 council deliberately seeding the diplomatic teams with personnel with recent anomalous trauma and no strong ties to baseline…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Oh man. I can't believe I missed this one before. Fucking fantastic. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Also I downvoted 2090 and don't have it in my headcanon. I just find the idea of the Foundation inventing an entire sport to contain an SCP to be absurd, and not in a believable way.

If you like it/consider it canon, just imagine that basketball developed independently of 2090 in the same way.

item.title: Title Change
item.content:encoded: Changed title from "The Ordinary Dimension" to "Normality," as it's less wordy and awkward.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
SCP-1485-A is primarily distinguishable from the Baseline world by its apparent total lack of anomalous or otherwise extranormal activity.

1485 Tim Duncan is just a basketball player. Nothing special.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: So, wait, if the Foundation doesn't exist there, then how come...?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
As I said in The Leak: 1485-A isn't our universe, not exactly. It's very similar to ours, but there are certain differences. I suppose the biggest one is that the SCP wiki does not exist (this is NOT a meta SCP).

I'm not sure exactly how else it's different, but one of the things I decided from the get-go was "no meta," and I think it worked out well. Fourth wall breaking has its place, but it isn't here.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
You suggesting that this "alternate universe" inside the portal is actually… the real reality world that we all inhabit, what with everything being normal and scientific and no mind/physics-exploding anomalies and the like?

too unrealistic, totally wacky…

still I +1

item.title: Re: Upvoted
item.content:encoded:
I'm with you on the hope that Earth-Prime doesn't get 'infected' with anomalies.

I sort of think of the SCP Universe as being a universe where the dimensional barriers are naturally thin…stuff gets in from all over the place.

Hell, there isn't even a CANON for the Foundation, there's so many universes overlapping it!

Many universes would be self-contained in their weirdness. Like, just aliens, or previously undiscovered but completely terrestrial animals, instead of interdimensional vending machines, or multiversal strip clubs.

Such universes might occasionally have pinprick holes punched into or out of them, but they'd still have those 'solid' barriers. A pinprick isn't going to make the Goodyear blimp pop or even appreciably *affect* it. I think universes tend the same way.

Of course, that doesn't mean the Earth-Prime government(s) wouldn't or couldn't start becoming *paranoid*….

But I much prefer that to 'this is the beginning of the universe collapsing under the weight of anomalies'.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is exactly where things were going in my head. For all we know, SCPs are running wild and it's actually (literal) hell on the other side. There are more than enough of them that could either keep the population under control with the illusion of happiness or just grab people before they cross back to here and swap out their memories of what happened.

That said, it does seem feasible even if it's exactly as written and there's no more mystery under the surface. Not being able to trust your senses or the basic laws of physics must put a strain on people.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like it. It's actually a fairly simple idea, but the human drama that it made happen in my head when I read it was moving. The interview strikes me as a bit stiff, though, and I'm having a hard time with the "We're sure that there's no anomalies besides the portal in the whole world" thing. I get that there don't seem to be any, and that they haven't been able to locate any SCP or GOI analogues, but they just seem too credulous to me. Neat idea, though, and I don't have any insight into how to fix the stuff that bugs me without changing the essential story.

No vote for now, gotta deconflict my head and see where I wind up.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This SCP is really well put together and an interesting read but a few things about it sort of rub me the wrong way.

-The defection angle is a nice concept and I do like it but I'm bothered by how en-masse it's presented as being. It makes sense that it might happen occasionally but the idea that so many people are going to throw way their entire life to become a homeless fugitive in another universe just because it probably doesn't have as much weeks shit going on strikes me as off.

-How readily the Foundation seems to accept the idea that there are no SCPs in the alternate universe seems a bit strange too. The description mentions that groups that actively hunt for the paranormal are generally derided and considered kooky by the public, but isn't that the same in the Foundationverse too? It seems perfectly plausible that the alternate government is lying to us or that, like wishun suggested the organization is even more clandestine than the foundation. Or it could be the other way and the organizations involved could be much more shoestring and low profile too.

Actually between the two of them I'm starting to think the real answer here is that either the universe itself or some sort of trap set up at their entrance to the portal has created some kind of cognitohazard that's causing Foundation personnel to so readily believe their universe is so much safer and more sane.

Also the whole time I was reading it (especially the zeppelin comment) I kept thinking "this is Fringe except we're the Other Side".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really like this one, but I wish somewhere in the article SOMEONE acknowledged that the fact that there is a return portal means that this universe is no longer completely free of skips. They have one. Just the one, but it's now their 001. The Foundation is full of smart people. Someone would notice and note this. Neutral voting for now, will have to see if it gets edited or if decide on a later read through if this seems less problematic than it does right now.

Despite that detail, however, do want to reiterate I love this one: I'd try to express why, but Photosynthetic already did it for me and so much more eloquently than I ever could. It's a twisting of the gut that only makes sense from the perspective of Baseline, and it works so well.

…I wonder if this is how skips arrived in Baseline?

Also, in a moment of "Huh" - I'd love to know how the Foundation convinced the governments of Earth-Prime they weren't just well armed nutjobs with compatable delusions? That'd be a Tale I'd like to read!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ehhhhhhhhhhhhhhhhhhhhhhhhhhhhhh I don't like it. While I realize this isn't _exactly_ "The Foundation discovering the REAL WORLD," it very well _could_ be, and that's certainly what it feels like. I don't think it belongs here, not like this. Downvote. Sorry.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Very nice. A staggering work of heartbreaking genius. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks for the praise! This is my highest rated article so far (other than the goddamn crossword), and I think it's because I hit the emotional notes I was aiming for. Like TL333 said, people who wish they lived in the Foundationverse would regret that wish if they ever actually went there.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I love it when people invent interpretations for my SCPs I hadn't even thought of.

Death of the Author is the best trope ever.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This article is a fantastic inversion of the whole SCP conceit. The story here is not about the SCP object itself, not at all, nor even about the Foundation. The story is about the people of the Foundation. It's about the horror and despair of living in a universe where you cannot trust anything. You can't trust your senses, the laws of biology or physics or psychology, you can't even trust fucking causality. And then you see a way out? And it's denied to you?

God, this grabbed me by the heartstrings and twisted.

+1, sir, and well-earned. I salute this article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
How can they be so sure there are no anomalies and no Foundation analog?
The alterna-US government says they know nothing about anomalies or a Foundation. But if Foundation-B was doing there jobs properly, the politicians wouldn't know, would they?
Maybe the only real difference between the two worlds is that the "Bizzaro" Foundation is much more effective.

Pure Conjecture: The true difference is that the Chaos Insurgency civil war went the other way, the faction that wanted to actively use anomalies won. The CI leadership were more gracious winners than they were losers in baseline, and the pure-containment faction was soon reintegrated into the newly re-focused "PCS Foundation". The reorganization is not even considered a coup in their history books, more like a change in corporate policy. The resulting integrated, balanced approach (use skips but be CAREFUL about it) means PCS is far more effective than SCP. They leave no trace of anomalies that are only barely hidden in baseline. They also are far less likely to need government cooperation. Outside a few half-formed paranoid suspicions at the FBI/CIA/NSA, no bureaucrat has any idea that such an organization exists much less what its true purpose would be.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: *whispers* I'm already writing something else up as an 001 proposal…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Very good, except that I think this might be a SCP-001 candidate.

item.title: Upvoted
item.content:encoded:
I actually rather like this— especially the memetic effect it has on people from the SCP-Earth universe. I was going to downvote until I read that last interview, which really put in to perspective how alien the SCP-Earth is, even to its own people.

Also, I seem to be alone here in that I hope that the other universe DOESN'T have any more phenomena. Having their universe' governments become secretive and paranoid about something that will never happen, and thus become hostile with the SCP-Foundation as a result of literally nothing, even as the Foundation's personnel seem to be walking off the job because of its effect on them… it seems to me that this is better than having them suddenly be swallowed up in a flood of SCPs after the portal was opened.

Indeed, I'd actually like to see an incident where someone attempted to carry an SCP object in to their universe and found themselves either physically or memetically incapable of doing so somehow…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I neutral-voted Romeo and Juliet, upvoted Othello.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
A fresh take on a broad idea. I've been waiting a long time for an 'our world' SCP article, and now that it exists, I want to form a canon about how these universes interact, with staff from the Foundationverse desperate to live a calm, stable existence, and people from their (our) timeline who wish to work for the Foundation.. and the shaky truce that holds the two universes together.

To the drawing board.

Oh yeah, I forgot. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I was set to no-vote, but the interview really drove this home. The grass is always greener, right? I know there are people here who'd love to go to the Foundation's universe because, holy cow, weird shit exists and wouldn't that be cool?

No. No it wouldn't. And those people don't want to be there either.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Nietzsche if you write a tale about this I will buy you a cookie

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
That wasn't precisely what I was going for, but you raise equally good points.

I was going more for the idea that, up until the Rift, they'd had no anomalies. The universe worked exactly the way it -should- according to our/their understanding and nothing more. This rift, though? That's just the beginning. They're going to have more anomalies now, and I sincerely doubt they'll be ones coming over from our side.

Or, if you prefer it with a bit more prose, in a way that universe was a sort of Eden. Unspoilt by the serpent, representing the darkness and sin that are the crimes against logic and nature we know as SCPs/skips/anomalies. Then the Rift happened. The snake was let loose. And the snake tempted Eve(us, under the assumption we could help them(Adam) or any other such noble cause into going through the other side.) And when their universe saw what happened…

They(Adam) are going to be cast out of Eden.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm not gonna lie, I thought this was a rewrite of scp-1322 at first. Still, I like where the story goes with this one. The interview does a pretty good job at showing me the kinds of things a SCP agent worries about. It can be stronger, though.

As much as I liked your take on this, I think you could elaborate more on what the people of 1485-A have to say. The article says there are minimal historical differences. This means they probably built sky-scrapers, went to the moon, and explored the deep sea just like us. They have just as much to lose as us, except they don't have a Foundation, and they're about 10,000 years late to the paranormal party. They are completely unprepared for this kind of thing, and it probably explains their attitude. They're probably thinking about what could happen if any more anomalies occurred.

Neutral vote for now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The Foundationverse is generally depicted as being superficially similar to our own in terms of history and world politics. I've always assumed the Foundation is/was competent enough to cover up any traces of anomalous activity and keep history on a path similar to ours. Though this world, and the Foundation world, aren't exactly ours…

Perhaps I should clarify this in The Leak…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I will concede that I was being a bit difficult last night in my objections. I guess what really bothered me was that this is a very good idea that I felt was cheapened by not being presented in a more unique manner. I have canceled my downvote and apologize if I annoyed anyone with my obstinancy. I DO understand modern SCP theory and the difference between cliche and themes. I just feel this passed the line of similar into nearly identical with regards to containment and description. The idea is a good one though and I applaud ObserverSeptember for it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I was expecting more divergence, That story about the butterfly and the hurricane and all.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: We're not?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like this article, but I disagree with the bolded text on "Sense" or the quotes around "different" in the interview log. That reads more like a script to a play or something than a psuedo-governmental official record. maybe make note of her emphasizing the word "sense" and fingerquoteing different or something.

Overall, +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
See, if this was actually explored in the article, with the other side's government going properly paranoid, blaming the Foundation with infecting their reality, and then one or two actual skips happen on their side, and they deal with it somehow, maybe not with creating a Foundation of their own because they saw ours and didn't like it, but instead doing something completely different to protect themselves against the coming anomalies, then the payoff would good enough for me to upvote.

</run-on-sentence>

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I don't think some here appreciate what's happened in this. We found a world with no anomalies, yes. Well, let me rephrase that.

We found a world's first anomaly.

This is the first time it's ever happened on their side. Which means things are breaking down there. For whatever reason, some way and some how…their universe is going down the same path the Foundation-verse did at some point. Let's assume, for the Foundation-timeline, that the first anomaly, whether we(the Foundation) were around to contain it or not, happened at year 0 CE. Just say it did for this thought experiment, alright?

As years pass, it starts to snowball, very slowly. More and more anomalies occur, ect, ect, ect Foundation formed sometime to deal with, yadda yadda.

Well, in that universe, this bridge that opened up, from their side to ours?

That's their Year 0.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This had to be done eventually, and it's been done pretty well. But like anqxyr, I want more of a payoff. Neutral vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I was expecting this to go along the lines of "and this ordinary world is actually real world in which you and me live and there's a website about SCPs and look how clever and meta I am", and I'm very glad it didn't, I despise that trope.

However, the payoff feels weak to me. Neutral vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I downvoted Hamlet.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
While this is getting off-topic pretty quickly… well, the fact is, Gattsune, every so often we have some user (usually new) who misunderstands why we say stuff is 'cliche' or 'too similar to XYZ other article'. This user tends to annoy pretty much everyone else. Especially when, for consistency purposes, they start downvoting many (often very good) SCP articles for the crime of being vaguely similar to some other article. Which is what usually happens. (It used to be worse when the Cliche List was actively maintained, because then such users would reference that.)

If the similarities bug you, fine. But it sounds like you're downvoting because you've misunderstood 'modern SCP theory'. Which is understandable, but it's also going to be really aggravating for everyone if you stick to your guns on that.

In other words:

However, I cannot in good conscience upvote a skip that is otherwise a near carbon copy of SCP-1322. It needs a lot more to stand on its own feet. -1

This is bad logic. This has similar story elements to Hartle, but those elements were never, ever original to Hartle. They're boilerplate, standard stuff for this kind of story, and in both SCPs, they're not the point.

It's like hating on tragedies because they require the main character to be admirable (a king, CEO, etc), go through a fall from grace due to hubris, and die in the end. SUCH CLICHES AMIRITE?

If you can't differentiate between story structure and cliche, you're going to have a very difficult time writing yourself. Because literally almost everything has been done already. Everything on the SCP wiki has also been done in some way somewhere else. Therefore, it's the execution that matters.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like where this went with the "alternate dimension" plot. It's like we're the weird ones.
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: When I say conflict I don't mean violence. The conflict Is that the Foundation doesn't want valuable agents with sensitive data defecting to a paranoid government that might decide to attack if they feel threatened. Which is what already played out in 1322. (Not the defecting agents part. The attack part.)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm constantly going into these things thinking "how 'extraordinary' are they gonna make THIS SCP?", and I was pleasantly surprised to find that this world was extraordinary by NOT being extraordinary, if that makes sense… Very well done.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
5. Conflict that necessitates sealing and guarding of portal.

That's not why the portal is being guarded.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I understand that. It would be fine if it didn't look like he had a checklist that he was ticking off as he wrote it.

1. Free floating rip in time space that allows matter and energy to travel through. Check
2. Contact with civilization of homo sapiens. Check
3. Diplomatic relations. Check
4. Failure of diplomatic relations. Check.
5. Conflict that necessitates sealing and guarding of portal. Check

If the article had just tweaked any of this to a small degree to not make it so damn similar in description and containment then I would upvoted happily. I love the story concept behind this article. It just reads exactly like 1322 in every other respect.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I would say, based on your "Blue Pool" example above, that you are missing the point of why some skips get downvoted as "cliche" or "too much like SCP-XXX" and others don't. You are 100% right that if an SCP tells the same story as an earlier one, or is easily recognized as "X from Y, SCP-ified" people will downvote, because you repeated the same story. Not because the objects are similar. This is a common misconception. Your "Blue Pool" would get downvoted, because it is telling the same story with some minor details changed.

In other words: Want to make a cliched item work? Tell a story about it that the reader is not expecting.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The example was off the top of my head. The core idea is the same though. It's a almost identical portal that goes to a different world. Even the government diplomacy subplot was almost exactly the same. With less nanomachines.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I have paid attention to modern SCP theory. A goodly percent of submitted SCP entries are rejected for being too similar to other skips. If I had a dollar for every time I have heard "overdone", "cliche", or "we already have one of those" when we down vote articles then I could take a nice vacation. We have guides that specifically state that we don't want copied ideas as new skips. Maybe I am confused or overreacting but I fail to see how this gets a pass when other articles that do the same thing don't and we castigate the authors for it.
